coredata.dc:title: Bioinspired fluorescent molecules realize super bright blue luminescence under sunlight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Qin, Ludan
Cao, Duxia
Guan, Ruifang
Cheng, Xiao
Zhou, Chuanjian
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, non-aromatic fluorescent materials have attracted widespread attention, but achieving a high quantum yield ( QY ) in their dilute solutions still remains a challenge. In this work, inspired by the Clustering-triggered emission (CTE) phenomenon of non-aromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids), we successfully designed a bioinspired molecule with a dipeptide-like structure and active silanol groups. The as-prepared bioinspired molecule can be hydrolyzed by simple heating to obtain the hydrolysate h-PDs , which can emit strong blue fluorescence in dilute solution. And this was the first time to realize the emission behavior of non-aromatic fluorescent materials under sunlight. The QY of h-PDs in aqueous solution and solid state were as high as 98.2% and 96.3%, respectively, which were attributed to the condensation of active silanol groups at the end of the molecule, and the existence of hydrogen bonds and N → Si coordination bonds that made small molecules cross-linked and aggregated into dense clusters. Finally, the application of h-PDs in the detection of intracellular and exogenous Hg2+ and Cys and luminescent films were also explored. Significantly, this work provides a design strategy for synthesizing high-fluorescence QY non-aromatic fluorescent materials, and verifies their potential application value in the fields of environment, biology and light-emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-aromatic
Fluorescence emission
Quantum yield
Clustering-triggered emission
Spatial electron delocalization system
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric determination of the general procedure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of h-PDs , (b) DLS particle size analysis of h-PDs , (c) GPC analysis of h-PDs , (d) infrared spectrum of AEAPMDS, CA-ADs and h-PDs , (e) 1H NMR of CA-ADs and h-PDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of h-PDs , (b) and (c) high-resolution Si 2p and N 1 s spectra of XPS, respectively, (d) N → Si coordination bond structure in h-PDs.

(a) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CA-ADs and h-PDs , (b) Photos of h-PDs in aqueous solution under sunlight, (c) Photographs of h-PDs solid under sunlight and 365 nm UV light, (d) and (e) are the emission spectra of fluorescent materials at different excitation wavelengths before and after hydrolysis, respectively, (f) Fluorescence intensity of CA-ADs and h-PDs under 365 nm UV lamp for different time (0 ∼ 12 h).
(a) SEM image of F-SMs, (b) Fluorescence spectrum of F-SMs dispersed in aqueous solution before and after hydrolysis.

(a) Selectivity of h-PDs solution to various ions (500 μM, blue bars), and interference of 500 μM of other ions with 500 μM Hg2+ (pink bars) in aqueous solution at pH 7.0. 1, blank; 2, Ca2+; 3, Cu2+; 4, Fe2+; 5, Fe3+; 6, Hg2+; 7, Mg2+ 8, Co2+; 9, Na+; 10, K+; 11, Al3+; 12, Zn2+; 13, Cd2+; 14, CO3 2−; 15, SO4 2−; 16, PO4 3−; 17, I−; 18, F−; 19, Br−. (b) Fluorescence response of the h-PDs after adding Hg2+ (0–400.0 μM), (c) Correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ (0–400.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of the h-PDs , (d) Semilogarithmic correlations between the concentrations of Hg2+ (0–180.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of the h-PDs , (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of the h-PDs , h-PDs with Hg2+ and Hg2+ solution, (f) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the h-PDs and h-PDs with Hg2+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Toxicity evaluation of h-PDs in A549 cells, (b) Image of h-PDs in A549 cells and detection of Hg2+ and Cys containing sulfhydryl amino acids.
Optical photograph of CA-ADs films on glass plates under visible (upper part) and UV light (360 nm, lower part).
Synthesis strategy of CA-ADs and h-PDs.

head.ce:title: Bioinspired fluorescent molecules realize super bright blue luminescence under sunlight

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, non-aromatic fluorescent materials have attracted widespread attention, but achieving a high quantum yield ( QY ) in their dilute solutions still remains a challenge. In this work, inspired by the Clustering-triggered emission (CTE) phenomenon of non-aromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids), we successfully designed a bioinspired molecule with a dipeptide-like structure and active silanol groups. The as-prepared bioinspired molecule can be hydrolyzed by simple heating to obtain the hydrolysate h-PDs , which can emit strong blue fluorescence in dilute solution. And this was the first time to realize the emission behavior of non-aromatic fluorescent materials under sunlight. The QY of h-PDs in aqueous solution and solid state were as high as 98.2% and 96.3%, respectively, which were attributed to the condensation of active silanol groups at the end of the molecule, and the existence of hydrogen bonds and N → Si coordination bonds that made small molecules cross-linked and aggregated into dense clusters. Finally, the application of h-PDs in the detection of intracellular and exogenous Hg2+ and Cys and luminescent films were also explored. Significantly, this work provides a design strategy for synthesizing high-fluorescence QY non-aromatic fluorescent materials, and verifies their potential application value in the fields of environment, biology and light-emitting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic fluorescent materials have a wide range of applications in many areas such as emissive devices, sensors, and bioimaging.[1-4] It is of great significance to explore the relationship between fluorescence properties and corresponding molecular structures to expand its application. Traditional luminescence paradigm describes the dynamics of delocalized electrons in some materials (i.e. usually aromatic/extended π-systems), which are generally assumed to exhibit luminescence properties by functioning as independent or singular molecular entities.[5-7] But they exhibit varying degrees of aggregation-caused emission quenching (ACQ) while aggregating.[8] This greatly limits their practical application at higher concentration. To break this limitation, Tang et al proposed a novel aggregation-induced emission (AIE) mechanism.[4,9-11] In other words, aromatic fluorescent molecules have intramolecular rotation and vibration, and only when such motion is blocked in aggregation state or solid state, can strong emission occur. Although these aromatic fluorescent materials with conjugated structures are widely used and fully studied, but their high toxicity and low water solubility are still difficult to solve.

Fortunately, in recent years, non-aromatic fluorescent materials in the absence of traditional/conventional (i.e. extended aromatic) luminophores that have excellent properties such as simple preparation process, good water solubility, good biocompatibility and inherent fluorescence emission have gradually attracted people's interest.[12-20] It has the potential to replace traditional fluorescent materials in certain application fields such as life sciences and environmental sciences. These new entities behaved concentration-enhanced emissions as compared to the traditional luminophores. Moreover, in many cases, the emission intensities of these entities were enhanced even in the solid state. In general, this new emerging paradigm refer to as non-traditional intrinsic luminescence (NTIL).[15] NTIL involves electron-rich hetero-atomic compositions and hetero-atomic functional groups, such as amines, amides, nitriles, imines, hydroxyls, carbonyls, and etc.[14,15,19,21-23] Usually, typical NTIL emission behaviors are caused by the aggregation of functional groups involving electron-rich hetero-atoms under multiple interactions. Electron-rich functional groups moieties provide unique sources of delocalizable electrons with no requirement of traditional aromatic or extended π-type structures.[23] Simply immobilizing these electron-rich functional groups moieties into assemblies/clusters by confinement strategies may force these confined assemblies into “hybridized, single quantum state-like clouds of delocalizable electrons”.[15,23] Indeed, these highly confined/immobilized assemblies may cause the heavy unprecedented NTIL emissions. Undoubtedly, this new photochemical phenomenon would exhibit a very bright/optimistic future, however, new scientific and application possibilities still require to be explored and implemented.

Although the fluorescence emission mechanism from NTIL is still under controversy, the proposal of each luminescence mechanism can inspire us to prepare more excellent luminescent molecules with similar structures. Recent study by Yuan et al.[20] showed that the intrinsic emission of nonaromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids) in concentrated solution or crystals is widespread through the mechanism of CTE. The key to CTE is the overlap of the electron cloud and subsequent rigidification of the conformation, because the unconventional chromophores (i.e. amino, carbonyl and hydroxyl) are very close.[24,25] This discovery opens up a new avenue toward the design of bioinspired fluorescent materials. Inspired by the fact that the CTE of amino acids only occur in their concentrated solution or crystals, we innovatively propose that if the structure of an amino acid-like molecule contains a specific functional group that under certain conditions can cause covalent reactions between the molecules to aggregate together, then it will produce extremely strong bioinspired fluorescence emission in dilute solution.

Many reports about unconventional luminescent silicon-containing polymers have attracted our attention.[26-34] Feng et al. reported a series of siloxane luminescent derivatives, and the carbonyl groups of these derivatives were tightly agglomerated through Si-O covalent bonds and N → Si coordination bonds, producing unconventional fluorescence emission.[30,33] Therefore, we considered that if the silicon hydroxyl with high reactivity and N-containing groups that can coordinate with Si were introduced into the bionic molecular structure, the hydrolysis and condensation between the silanol groups and the existence of the N → Si coordination bond may cause the aggregation of small molecules to produce a CTE effect, which can produce strong fluorescence emission in dilute solution. In this work, a bioinspired fluorescent molecule ( CA-ADs ) using citric acid and N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMDS) as raw materials was successfully designed. The molecule had a bionic structure similar to that of a dipeptide, as well as silanol groups that can continue to react. The spherical hydrolysate h-PDs with their liquid and solid fluorescent QY up to 98.2% and 96.3% were obtained by simply dissolving the bioinspired fluorescent molecules in water and heating at 100 ℃ for 10 min. Such a high QY enables it to emit bright blue fluorescence under sunlight. Various characterization test results showed that heating, stirring, and the presence of hydrogen bonds and N → Si coordination bonds induced molecular hydrolysis and condensation and cross-linking to form a compact structure. The powerful spatial electron delocalization system and the suppression of non-radiative transitions were the cause of the high fluorescence QY .

Citric acid, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyl dimethoxyl silane (AEAPMDS) and thionyl chloride were all purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). Tetrahydrofuran, petroleum ether and n-hexane were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.

Ultraviolet–visible and photoluminescence spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer and a Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer (Horiba JY, France). Dilute solutions were placed in quartz cuvettes (1.0 cm path length) and tested by infrared fluorescence combined measurement system (Nanolog FluoroLog-3-2-iHR320). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield was measured by NanoLog FL3-2iHR infrared fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba JY, USA). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured in D2O on a MercuryPlus-400 spectrometer (Swiss). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on NICOLET-iS50 spectrometer to characterize chemical groups.

The experiment process can be divided into two steps.[35] First, citric acid chloride was synthesized. 2 g (0.01 mol) citric acid was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran in an ice bath, then 2.4 ml (0.033 mol) thionyl chloride was added dropwise and stirred for 12 h. The above product was mixed with n-hexane and rotary evaporated at 0 °C for three times to remove impurities and byproducts to obtain purified citric acid chloride. Second, 0.62 g (0.0025 mol) citric acid chloride was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran, 1.8 ml (0.0084 mol) AEAPMDS was added to citric acid chloride solution and kept stirring for 3 h. And CA-ADs was finally obtained after washing with petroleum ether for several times. The structure of CA-ADs was confirmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR ( Figure S1 ) and HRMS ( Figure S2 ).

0.1 g CA-ADs was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water, then the solution was transferred into a glass bottle and heated at 100 °C in oil bath for 10 min. Finally, h-PDs was obtained by removing the water under vacuum.
The cell toxicity of h-PDs was tested with the MTT method. A549 cells were cultured referring to reference process. [36,37]

Six glass plates were all soaked in sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution (10 M) for 5 days, and washed thoroughly with deionized water. Then these plates were put into 2 ml CA-ADs solution of different concentration respectively at 80 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric determination of the general procedure
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence and absorption tests were performed in the deionized water as solvent at room temperature. In the analysis of photophysical properties, the concentration of h-PDs was adjusted to 5.0 mg/mL unless otherwise stated. In the ion detection experiment, the concentration of h-PDs was adjusted to 0.5 mg/mL. In addition, anionic and cationic aqueous solutions having a concentration of 0.01 M were respectively prepared using a sodium salt and a hydrochloride salt.

The QY of the compounds was measured according to an established procedure. The integrated fluorescence intensity (excited at 360 nm) and the absorbance (less than 0.1 at 360 nm) was compared with those of quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, Φ = 0.54). The equation is as follows:
Φ x
=
Φ s
(
I x
I s
)
(
η x
η s
)
2

Where Φ is the QY , “I” is the slop from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance and “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “S” and “X” refers to the standards with known QY and the compounds, respectively.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the bioinspired molecule CA-ADs with a dipeptide structure, amino groups and silanol groups, was synthesized by the acid chloride method. As expected, a hydrolysate h-PDs of high fluorescent QY that can emit strong blue bioinspired fluorescence under sunlight was obtained by simply heating and hydrolyzing the CA-ADs dissolved in an aqueous medium. Fig. 1
a shows the SEM image of the as-prepared h-PDs . As shown in Fig. 1 a, h-PDs were spherical, with excellent dispersion and a particle size distribution of 80 ∼ 140 nm. During the hydrolysis of CA-ADs , dehydration condensation and cross-linking between molecules occurred, resulting in the spherical aggregation of h-PDs . The DLS test results in Fig. 1 b show that h-PDs were cluster aggregates around 140 nm, which was consistent with the results in Fig. 1 a. GPC analysis was performed on h-PDs to explore the degree of molecular condensation (Fig. 1 c). It can be seen from Fig. 1 c, h-PDs showed a narrow molecular distribution, with a polydispersity of 2.48, a weight average molecular weight ( Mw ) of 14899 g/mol, and a number average molecular weight ( Mn ) of 6019 g/mol. However, as a whole, each nanoparticle should have a molecular weight in the hundreds of thousands or even millions. It is likely that the samples passed through the 0.22 μM filter membrane before testing and were filtered out, and thus only a few fractions of unclustered oligomer molecules were detected. Therefore, it could be inferred that the terminal silanol groups of CA-ADs molecules dehydrated and condensed under heating, and the flexible siloxane oligomer molecules were obtained consequently. Then the molecular chains were cross-linked and agglomerated into nanoclusters. The process of condensation and agglomeration may occur simultaneously. The condensation of silanol groups can be verified from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1 d). Compared with CA-ADs , the tensile vibration peaks (3200 ∼ 3400 cm−1) derived from OH and NH in the h-PDs structure were significantly weakened, which was due to the reduction of OH caused by the condensation of silanol, consistent with the GPC results. The vibration modes I and II of amide were located at 1653 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1, respectively, and their chemical shifts were the same as those of peptide bonds, which proved that amide bonds participated in the formation hydrogen bonds.[12,38] In addition, CA-ADs and h-PDs can only dissolve in the water phase, which may be attributed to the large number of hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl and amide groups in their structures. Finally, the NMR changes before and after hydrolysis were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the peak near 0.1 ppm derives from the methyl group at the end of CA-ADs . The peaks at 0.48, 1.53, and 2.78 ppm can be attributed to the three methylene groups between the silicon atom and the secondary amine. The peak between 2.32 and 2.49 ppm is attributed to the methylene group of the citric acid part. The broad peak at 3.0 ppm is ascribed to the methylene group between the amide group and the secondary amino group. After heated hydrolysis, the proton peak (2.78 and 3.00 ppm) of –CH2- near the amide group and the secondary amine group shifted to the lower field (2.87 and 3.16 ppm) significantly. It is suggested that the molecule containing some functional groups in the structure, such as amide groups, hydroxyl groups, and amino groups, will produce more intermolecular hydrogen bonds after aggregation, resulting in a reduction in the electron cloud density, as previous research reported. [39,40] A large number of hydrogen bonds in the structure not only facilitate the cross-linking and aggregation between molecules, but also enhance the rigidity of the molecules themselves and inhibit non-radiative transitions. [16,41]

Feng et al. found that in polysiloxane derivatives, the presence of the N → Si coordination bond can promote the aggregation of carbonyl groups to produce unconventional fluorescence emission.[30,33] In this work, XPS was used to verify the existence of the N → Si coordination bond. As shown in Fig. 2
a, C, O, N and Si were the main constituent elements of h-PDs . In addition, the Si 2p and N 1 s peaks were located at 101.8 eV and 399.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 b and c), and the positions of these peaks were consistent with the positions when the N → Si coordination bond existed.[27,30,33] The five-membered ring structure of the N → Si coordination bond in h-PDs is shown in Fig. 2 d. The existence of the N → Si coordination bond not only promoted the self-assembly of h-PDs and made aggregation easier; but the formation of the five-membered ring also restricted molecular rotation and inhibited non-radiative transitions.

First, the changes in the absorption and fluorescence properties of CA-ADs and h-PDs were analyzed. In the absorption spectrum, there was a broad absorption peak at 300 ∼ 400 nm in both fluorescent materials, which are attributed to the n–π* transition caused by the agglomeration of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.[21,26,27] And the difference was that the absorption peak intensity of h-PDs was significantly higher than that of CA-ADs (Fig. 3
a), which is obviously attributed to agglomeration caused by hydrolysis. The optimal excitation and optimal emission wavelengths of the hydrolysate h-PDs were also significantly enhanced, but the peak position did not change (Fig. 3 a). The QY of CA-ADs aqueous solution was 40.7%, while the QY of h-PDs aqueous solution was as high as 98.2%, which was much higher than some organic conjugated fluorescent molecules and quantum dots. Such a high QY caused the colorless and transparent aqueous solution of h-PDs to emit bright blue fluorescence under sunlight (Fig. 3 b), which further confirmed the reliability of this design. More importantly, the QY of h-PDs solid powder was still as high as 96.3% (Fig. 3 c and S4), which was far from being achieved by ACQ and most AIE fluorescent materials. To the best of our knowledge, the liquid and solid luminous efficiency of h-PDs designed in this work was the highest reported among non-aromatic fluorescent materials.[24-26,37] In addition, before and after hydrolysis, the emission wavelength of these two fluorescent materials was stable at about 450 nm, and the emission wavelength remained unchanged with the change of excitation wavelength (280 nm-440 nm) (Fig. 3 d and e). Excellent light stability is the prerequisite for the use of fluorescent materials. Then the changes in the photobleaching resistance of two fluorescent materials under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation were studied. As shown in Fig. 3 f, after 12 h of irradiation with 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence intensity of CA-ADs was reduced by 4/5, while the fluorescence intensity of h-PDs was still stable at about 80%, far better than ordinary organic fluorescent materials,[35,36,42] which may be due to self-protection caused by the aggregate structure. In short, the extremely high liquid and solid luminous efficiency of h-PDs and excellent photobleaching resistance means that they can be applied in more research fields, such as LED light-emitting devices.

The luminescence mechanism was analyzed by absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. The best excitation peak of the two fluorescent materials were corresponding to the broad absorption peak at around 360 nm, and the absorption peak should be derived from the n-π* electronic transition.[14,26,27] According to literature reports,[25,27] after clusters of non-traditional fluorophores (amino, carboxyl, amide, and hydroxyl) in NTIL materials aggregate, lone pairs of nitrogen and oxygen electrons overlap in the clusters to form a spatial electron delocalization system, and then induce n-π* electronic transition. We speculate that the aggregation process can improve the fluorescence QY through two aspects. On the one hand, non-traditional fluorophores (amino, carboxyl, amide, and hydroxyl) get closer to each other, resulting in a stronger spatial electron delocalization system. On the other hand, the rotation and vibration of the molecules are more strongly restricted after aggregation, thereby suppressing the non-radiative transition and enhancing the fluorescence emission.

According to related literature reports,[12,43] heating and oxidation can enhance the fluorescence emission of non-aromatic fluorescent materials. As shown in Fig. 4
a, in order to clarify the contribution of the oxidation process and the aggregation process to the fluorescence enhancement, fluorescent silica microspheres (F-SMs) were obtained by confining the fluorescent molecule CA-ADs to the silica structure via the Stöber method.[35,44] Then the same hydrolysis conditions were adopted to process F-SMs and analyze the changes in fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the fluorescence enhancement of F-SMs was much lower than that produced by hydrolysis of CA-ADs molecule. Therefore, it can be concluded that the delocalization system generated by the aggregation and the suppression of non-radiative transition contributed far more to the high fluorescence emission than oxidation.

In some recent work, a series of novel and interesting luminescent materials have been prepared with citric acid and molecules containing primary amine groups as raw materials through multi-step dehydration-condensation processes under high temperature and vacuum conditions.[45-48] It is considered that the luminescence of such luminescent compounds (derivatives of ring-fused dihydroimidiazolo 2-pyridone or tetrahydropyridine) originates from the conjugate part of the structure.[45,46] However, we have synthesized a product by a simple amidation reaction that was similar to the product of the first dehydration-condensation step of their work, but without any conjugate components in the structure. It is worth noting that although their structures are different, their fluorescence properties show remarkable similarity, mainly including three aspects. (1) the strong absorption band and emission band are mainly in the range of 300 nm ∼ 400 nm and 380 nm ∼ 550 nm, respectively; (2) fluorescence QY is generally higher; (3) showing the nature of concentration-enchanced emission. In fact, the similarity of these fluorescence properties means that the luminescence mechanism may be similar as well. Therefore, we suspect that the luminescence of luminescent compounds containing conjugated structures in these works may still belong to NTIL, which of course needs to be verified by more detailed and systematic studies in the future.

The effects of different concentrations of h-PDs in aqueous solutions on the fluorescence properties were studied in detail. As shown in Figure S5 , the fluorescence of the h-PDs aqueous solution increased obviously with the increase of concentration (0.05 ∼ 20.00 mg/mL), showing the nature of concentration-enhanced emission. The concentration-enhanced emission properties of unconventional luminescent materials have been widely reported,[12,26] and this property, which is completely opposite to the ACQ property, means that it can be applied in more fields. The changes in absorption and fluorescence spectra at different concentrations were also studied ( Figure S6 ). Interestingly, the intensity of the maximum absorption peak of h-PDs increased linearly with the increase of concentration, while the increase of the fluorescence peak intensity was non-linear, and this phenomenon should be attributed to the fluorescence inner-filter effect.[12,49]

Most non-aromatic fluorescent materials can be quenched by one or several ions, which inspired us to explore the application of h-PDs in ion detection. In this work, the fluorescence response of h-PDs (0.5 mg/mL, aqueous solution) to multiple ions (500 μM) was studied. And the results are shown in Fig. 5 a. Both Fe3+ and Hg2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence emission of h-PDs (blue bars). Fortunately, sodium hexametaphosphate can perfectly shield Fe3+ without affecting the process of responding to Hg2+.[35] In addition, the binding between h-PDs and Hg2+ is not significantly affected by other ions (Fig. 5
a, pink bars). Then we analyzed the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of h-PDs and the concentration of Hg2+ through titration experiments and found that the fluorescence intensity of h-PDs gradually decreased as the concentration of Hg2+ (0 ∼ 400 μM) increased (Fig. 5 b). Fig. 5 c and d show the semi-logarithmic relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of h-PDs and the concentration of Hg2+. It was obvious that the concentration of Hg2+ between 0.1 and 180 μM satisfies the linear equation Log (I0 / I) = 0.00265C (R2 = 0.99883, n = 13), where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg2+, C represents the concentration of Hg2+, and the calculated detection limit is 0.12 μM.[12,50]

Then, the mechanism of Hg2+ quenching by h-PDs was studied by combining the changes of absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 5e, the absorption peak of h-PDs near 360 nm showed a significant red shift after adding Hg2+, which can be attributed to the structural changes caused by the combination of Hg2+ and h-PDs .[51,52] The results in Fig. 5f show that the fluorescence lifetime of h-PDs after binding to Hg2+ decreases from 12.92 ns to 7.80 ns, indicating that there is a static quenching process between Hg2+ and h-PDs .[52-54]

To further study the fluorescence “turn on” sensing strategy of h-PDs , various amino acids were added to the h-PDs solution quenched by Hg2+. And the results showed that three amino acids containing sulfhydryl groups (Cys, Hcy, GSH) can significantly recover the fluorescence emission of h-PDs ( Figure S7 ). According to relevant literature reports,[53,55,56] this is because the sulfhydryl groups on the three amino acid molecules can form heavy metal-sulfhydryl bonds with Hg2+, which can restore fluorescence emission by stripping off Hg2+ on the surface of h-PDs . Therefore, h-PDs can be used as potential fluorescent probes for detecting Hg2+ and these three sulfhydryl-containing amino acids.

To verify the application of h-PDs in the field of biology, its cytotoxicity was first studied. As shown in Fig. 6
a, when the concentration of h-PDs was as high as 0.1 mg/mL, the survival rate of A549 cells was above 80%. Continue to increase the concentration to 20.0 mg/mL, the survival rate was still around 80%, indicating that h-PDs had low toxicity and great application potential in the field of cell imaging. In Fig. 6 b, A1, A2, and A3 are bright-field, dark-field, and bright-dark-field overlapping images of A549 cells that had been removed sulfhydryl amino acids under a confocal microscope, respectively. B1, B2, and B3 are the images of the cells cultured with h-PDs for 2 h. It can be seen from the picture that the compounds enter the cytoplasm and emit bright fluorescence. C1, C2, and C3 are the images of the above cells after being cultured in Hg2+ solution for 2 h, respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence is almost completely quenched. Then put the above cells in Cys solution and culture for 2 h, it can be seen that the quenched fluorescence is restored (D2). The above experimental results prove that h-PDs can be used for cell imaging and can detect Hg2+ and sulfhydryl-containing amino acids in cells.

The hydrolyzation triggered significant enhancement of the fluorescence, and the extremely high quantum yield of the obtained powder indicates that the CA-ADs system can be used as robust solid fluorescent materials, similar to the most attractive AIE material. As a demo of this idea, we deposited the CA-ADs onto a glass plate. The glass plate was firstly treated in NaOH solution and then was immersed in the CA-ADs solution. After heating at 100 °C for 2 h, a film of CA-ADs was covalently coated on the glass which endowed the glass with strong fluorescence. Figure S8 shows that the fluorescence intensity increased as the concentration of the CA-ADs increased, but the transparency of the glass was not affected (Fig. 7 ). Strikingly, the film was so robust that it was stable even under rigorous ultrasonication for 30 min. This means that these CA-ADs coated glasses have great potential in LED fabrication or related fields.

In short, by designing the molecular structure, a bioinspired fluorescent molecule with a dipeptide structure and active silanol was synthesized. The hydrolyzed product h-PDs with fluorescence QY of up to 98.2% and 96.3% under liquid and solid conditions were obtained by simple heating and hydrolysis, and the stimulated emission of h-PDs in dilute solution under sunlight was realized. The test results show that the condensation of active silanol groups at the molecular end, the presence of hydrogen bonds and N → Si coordination bonds make small molecules cross-linked and aggregated into a dense cluster structure, and the reasons for the high QY can be concluded to the extremely strong spatial electron delocalization system and the suppression of non-radiative transitions. Finally, the application of h-PDs to Hg2+ detection, intracellular and exogenous Hg2+ and Cys detection and luminescent films have been explored.

Hao Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Ludan Qin: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Duxia Cao: Investigation, Data curation. Ruifang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiao Cheng: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chuanjian Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This investigation was sponsored financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51903139), the key research and development program of Shandong Province (2021ZLGX01, 2019JZZY010355 and 2019JZZY010344) and Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.10.096.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
